questions.stem: What distinguishes a generative AI model from a discriminative AI model?

options.A: Generative models only work on text; discriminative models only work on images
options.B: Generative models learn the joint distribution P(X,Y) and can produce new data samples; discriminative models learn P(Y|X) — the conditional probability of a label given input — and classify but do not generate new content
options.C: Generative models are always larger than discriminative models
options.D: Discriminative models require no training data

questions.explanation: Discriminative models (logistic regression, SVM, BERT for classification) learn decision boundaries — P(Y|X). Generative models (GPT, DALL-E, VAEs, GANs) learn the underlying data distribution P(X) or P(X,Y) and can sample new instances. This enables text generation, image synthesis, audio creation, and drug molecule design.
questions.source: Goodfellow et al. Deep Learning. MIT Press, 2016. Ch.16. https://www.deeplearningbook.org; Ng & Jordan. On Discriminative vs Generative Classifiers. NeurIPS, 2001.

questions.stem: What is a Large Language Model (LLM)?

options.A: A database storing large volumes of text documents
options.B: A deep learning model — typically a Transformer — trained on massive text corpora with billions of parameters, capable of generating, summarising, translating, and reasoning about text
options.C: A rule-based grammar checker for word processors
options.D: A small specialised model trained on domain-specific legal text only

questions.explanation: LLMs: Transformer-based neural networks trained on web-scale text (hundreds of billions to trillions of tokens). Examples: GPT-4 (~1.8T parameters estimated), LLaMA 3 (8B–405B), Gemini 1.5, Claude 3. Capabilities emerge at scale: few-shot learning, chain-of-thought reasoning, code generation — behaviours not explicitly trained for.
questions.source: Vaswani et al. Attention Is All You Need. NeurIPS, 2017. https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762; Wei et al. Emergent Abilities of LLMs. TMLR, 2022. https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.07682

questions.stem: Why does the Transformer architecture enable LLMs to handle long-range dependencies in text better than recurrent neural networks (RNNs)?

options.A: Transformers process text faster because they use fewer parameters than RNNs
options.B: Self-attention allows every token to directly attend to every other token in O(1) steps regardless of distance; RNNs must pass information through sequential hidden states — causing gradient vanishing over long sequences
options.C: Transformers only work on short sequences; RNNs handle longer text
options.D: RNNs and Transformers have identical long-range dependency handling

questions.explanation: RNN path length between tokens i and j: O(n) sequential steps — gradients decay exponentially. Transformer self-attention: O(1) direct connection between any two tokens via Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√dₖ)V. Cost is O(n²d) per layer — quadratic in sequence length — but modern techniques (FlashAttention, sparse attention) manage this. This is why GPT-4 can maintain coherence across 128K+ token contexts.
questions.source: Vaswani et al. NeurIPS, 2017; Hochreiter & Schmidhuber. LSTM. Neural Computation, 1997.

questions.stem: What is the pre-training objective of GPT-style (decoder-only) LLMs, and how does it differ from BERT's training objective?

options.A: Both GPT and BERT use identical masked language modelling objectives
options.B: GPT uses causal (autoregressive) language modelling — predict the next token given all previous tokens, left-to-right. BERT uses masked language modelling (MLM) — predict randomly masked tokens using bidirectional context. GPT is generative; BERT is optimised for understanding tasks
options.C: BERT predicts the next token; GPT predicts masked tokens
options.D: GPT uses supervised classification; BERT uses reinforcement learning

questions.explanation: GPT (decoder-only): P(xₜ|x₁,...,xₜ₋₁) — next-token prediction, causal attention mask. Enables open-ended generation. BERT (encoder-only): 15% of tokens masked → predict them using left AND right context. Bidirectional — excellent for classification, NER, QA. GPT-style: generate. BERT-style: understand. T5/BART: encoder-decoder — translate, summarise.
questions.source: Devlin et al. BERT. NAACL, 2019. https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805; Radford et al. GPT-2. OpenAI, 2019. https://openai.com/research/language-unsupervised

questions.stem: You need to build a customer support chatbot that answers questions about a company's product documentation (500 PDF pages). You have a limited budget and cannot fine-tune an LLM. Which architecture should you use?

options.A: Train a new LLM from scratch on the 500 pages
options.B: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): embed the documentation into a vector database, retrieve the top-k relevant chunks at query time, and inject them into the LLM prompt as context — combining retrieval precision with LLM fluency without fine-tuning
options.C: Use keyword search only — LLMs are not needed for document Q&A
options.D: Fine-tune BERT on the 500 pages for generation tasks

questions.explanation: RAG (Lewis et al., 2020): (1) chunk documents → embed with text-embedding-3 or similar → store in vector DB (Pinecone, FAISS, Chroma); (2) at query time, embed query → retrieve top-k chunks by cosine similarity; (3) inject chunks into LLM prompt → generate grounded answer. Advantages: no fine-tuning cost, knowledge updatable without retraining, citations possible. Used by Bing AI, Perplexity, and most enterprise AI assistants.
questions.source: Lewis et al. Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP. NeurIPS, 2020. https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401

questions.stem: What is RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) and which model first made it widely known?

options.A: A technique for training robots to walk using human motion capture data
options.B: A training pipeline where human raters rank model outputs, a reward model is trained on those rankings, and the LLM is fine-tuned via PPO to maximise the reward — aligning outputs with human preferences. Made widely known by InstructGPT/ChatGPT (OpenAI, 2022)
options.C: A method for compressing LLMs to run on mobile devices
options.D: A technique for filtering toxic content from training datasets

questions.explanation: RLHF pipeline (Christiano et al., 2017; Ouyang et al., 2022): (1) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on high-quality demonstrations; (2) human raters rank pairs of model outputs → train reward model; (3) PPO (Proximal Policy Optimisation) fine-tunes the LLM to maximise reward while staying close to the SFT model (KL divergence penalty). InstructGPT showed RLHF dramatically improved helpfulness and reduced harmful outputs vs GPT-3.
questions.source: Ouyang et al. Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback. NeurIPS, 2022. https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.02155

questions.stem: What is parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT), and which technique is most widely used?

options.A: Training all parameters of an LLM on a new task from scratch
options.B: Fine-tuning methods that update only a small fraction of model parameters — reducing compute and memory cost. The most widely used technique is LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation), which injects trainable low-rank matrices into each Transformer layer while freezing the original weights
options.C: Reducing model size by removing layers before deployment
options.D: Compressing the vocabulary to reduce embedding matrix size

questions.explanation: Full fine-tuning a 70B model requires 70B × 4 bytes × optimiser states ≈ 500GB+ GPU memory. LoRA (Hu et al., 2021): freeze W, add ΔW = AB where A∈ℝ^(d×r), B∈ℝ^(r×k), r≪d. Trains only 0.1–1% of parameters. QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) quantises base model to 4-bit, enabling 70B fine-tuning on a single 48GB GPU. Industry standard for domain adaptation.
questions.source: Hu et al. LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models. ICLR, 2022. https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.09685; Dettmers et al. QLoRA. NeurIPS, 2023.

questions.stem: Why do LLMs 'hallucinate' — generate plausible-sounding but factually incorrect statements — and what are the main mitigation strategies?

options.A: Hallucinations occur because LLMs are intentionally programmed to be creative
options.B: LLMs are trained to predict the next most likely token, not to verify factual accuracy. They interpolate from training patterns — generating fluent text that fits the context even when no reliable training signal exists for a specific fact. Mitigations: RAG (grounding in retrieved sources), chain-of-thought prompting, fine-tuning on factual data, calibration, and confidence scoring
options.C: Hallucinations only occur in smaller models and disappear at scale
options.D: Hallucinations are caused by incorrect tokenisation

questions.explanation: Root cause: next-token prediction objective maximises fluency, not factuality. LLMs have no explicit knowledge store — facts are implicitly encoded in weights. When queried on uncommon facts, low-frequency training signal, or post-training-cutoff events, the model 'confabulates' plausible continuations. Scaling helps but does not eliminate hallucination (GPT-4 still hallucinates ~20% on challenging factual benchmarks). RAG is the most reliable mitigation.
questions.source: Maynez et al. On Faithfulness and Factuality in Abstractive Summarisation. ACL, 2020; Ji et al. Survey of Hallucination in NLG. ACM Computing Surveys, 2023. https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.03629

questions.stem: How does prompt engineering differ from fine-tuning as a method for adapting LLM behaviour to a specific task?

options.A: They are identical — prompt engineering and fine-tuning produce the same results
options.B: Prompt engineering: craft input instructions/examples to guide model behaviour without updating weights — fast, cheap, flexible, but limited by context window and prompt brittleness. Fine-tuning: update model weights on task-specific data — higher performance, consistent behaviour, no prompt overhead, but requires data, compute, and risks catastrophic forgetting
options.C: Fine-tuning is always cheaper and faster than prompt engineering
options.D: Prompt engineering modifies model weights; fine-tuning does not

questions.explanation: Prompt engineering (zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought): no GPU required, rapid iteration, works with API-accessed closed models. Limitations: performance ceiling, prompt sensitivity, context window costs. Fine-tuning: surpasses few-shot for specialised tasks when 100s-1000s of examples available. LoRA/QLoRA make it accessible. Best practice: start with prompt engineering, fine-tune when performance gap is unacceptable.
questions.source: Brown et al. GPT-3: Few-Shot Learners. NeurIPS, 2020. https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165; Wei et al. Chain-of-Thought Prompting. NeurIPS, 2022.

questions.stem: A legal firm wants an LLM to draft contracts using jurisdiction-specific clauses. They have 2,000 labelled contract examples. Design the optimal adaptation strategy.

options.A: Use zero-shot prompting — no adaptation needed for legal text
options.B: (1) Start with a capable base model (GPT-4o, LLaMA 3 70B); (2) apply LoRA fine-tuning on the 2,000 contract examples with instruction format; (3) augment with RAG over a jurisdiction clause library for grounding; (4) evaluate with domain experts on contract accuracy, clause completeness, and jurisdictional correctness; (5) add human-in-the-loop review before any contract is sent
options.C: Train a new LLM from scratch on the 2,000 examples
options.D: Use keyword substitution templates — LLMs are not suitable for legal drafting

questions.explanation: 2,000 examples: sufficient for LoRA fine-tuning (instruction-format SFT), not enough for full fine-tuning. RAG over clause library grounds outputs in verified legal text — critical for jurisdiction accuracy. Human review is mandatory for legal documents (professional liability, hallucination risk). This is the standard enterprise legal AI architecture (Harvey AI, CoCounsel).
questions.source: Hu et al. LoRA. ICLR, 2022; Lewis et al. RAG. NeurIPS, 2020.

mini_quizzes.title: MQ-3 · Prompting Techniques and LLM Capabilities

questions.stem: What is chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting?

options.A: A technique that chains multiple LLMs together in a pipeline
options.B: A prompting strategy where the model is instructed or shown examples of step-by-step reasoning before producing a final answer — significantly improving performance on multi-step arithmetic, logical, and commonsense reasoning tasks
options.C: A method for compressing long prompts into shorter ones
options.D: A technique for connecting an LLM to external APIs

questions.explanation: CoT (Wei et al., NeurIPS 2022): adding 'Let's think step by step' or few-shot reasoning examples causes LLMs to externalise intermediate reasoning steps before answering. On GSM8K (grade school maths), CoT improved GPT-3 accuracy from 17% to 58%. Works best in models >100B parameters. Variants: zero-shot CoT ('Let's think step by step'), few-shot CoT (worked examples), self-consistency CoT (majority vote across multiple reasoning paths).
questions.source: Wei et al. Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in LLMs. NeurIPS, 2022. https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903

questions.stem: What does the 'temperature' parameter control in LLM text generation?

options.A: The physical temperature of the GPU during inference
options.B: The randomness of token sampling: low temperature (→0) makes output deterministic and repetitive; high temperature (→2) increases diversity and creativity but risks incoherence. Controls the sharpness of the softmax probability distribution over next tokens
options.C: The maximum number of tokens the model can generate
options.D: The speed of text generation measured in tokens per second

questions.explanation: Sampling: logits → divide by temperature T → softmax → sample. T→0: argmax (greedy, deterministic). T=1: standard softmax. T>1: flatter distribution, more diversity. Top-p (nucleus) sampling (Holtzman et al., 2020): sample from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p — more coherent than pure temperature sampling. For factual tasks: T=0–0.3. For creative tasks: T=0.7–1.2.
questions.source: Holtzman et al. The Curious Case of Neural Text Degeneration. ICLR, 2020. https://arxiv.org/abs/1904.09751

questions.stem: Why does few-shot prompting improve LLM task performance compared to zero-shot prompting?

options.A: Few-shot prompting updates the model's weights at inference time
options.B: Providing input-output examples in the prompt activates in-context learning — the model infers the task format, output style, and reasoning pattern from the examples without any gradient updates, effectively 'conditioning' the generative distribution on the demonstrated pattern
options.C: Few-shot prompting reduces hallucinations to zero
options.D: Few-shot examples are stored in the model's long-term memory

questions.explanation: In-context learning (ICL): LLMs implicitly implement a form of gradient descent in the forward pass (Akyürek et al., 2022). Examples narrow the predictive distribution to the demonstrated pattern. GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) showed dramatic few-shot gains on translation, QA, and classification — matching fine-tuned models in some tasks using 8–32 examples. Limitation: sensitive to example order, selection, and label balance.
questions.source: Brown et al. GPT-3. NeurIPS, 2020. https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165; Akyürek et al. What Learning Algorithm is In-Context Learning? ICLR, 2023.

questions.stem: What is a 'system prompt' in an LLM API, and why is it important for product deployments?

options.A: A system prompt is a hardware instruction that optimises GPU memory allocation
options.B: A system prompt is a hidden instruction prepended to every conversation that sets the model's persona, behaviour constraints, output format, and domain scope — invisible to end users but controlling how the model interprets and responds to all subsequent user messages
options.C: A system prompt is a user-visible welcome message displayed in chatbot UIs
options.D: A system prompt automatically fine-tunes the model for the deployment context

questions.explanation: System prompts (OpenAI API: 'role: system') define: persona ('You are a helpful medical assistant'), constraints ('Never provide dosing advice'), format ('Always respond in JSON'), and scope ('Only answer questions about our product'). Critical for safety (jailbreak resistance), consistency, and brand voice. Well-designed system prompts are treated as trade secrets by major AI product companies.
questions.source: OpenAI. Chat Completions API Documentation. 2024. https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/text-generation

questions.stem: A user reports that an LLM-powered coding assistant confidently suggested code containing a security vulnerability (SQL injection risk). The vendor says the model 'performs well on benchmarks.' Evaluate this response and propose a remediation.

options.A: Benchmark performance is sufficient — no action required
options.B: Benchmark performance on general coding tasks does not measure security-specific correctness. Proposed remediations: (1) add a security-focused system prompt instructing the model to flag potential vulnerabilities; (2) integrate static analysis tools (Semgrep, CodeQL) as a post-generation validation layer; (3) fine-tune on secure coding datasets (CWE examples); (4) implement human code review for security-sensitive code paths; (5) update benchmarks to include security-specific evaluations (SecurityEval)
options.C: Ask users to manually check all generated code for security issues
options.D: Switch to a rule-based code generator with no LLM component

questions.explanation: LLMs trained on public code inherit vulnerabilities present in that code (Pearce et al., 2022 found GitHub Copilot produced vulnerable code in ~40% of security-relevant scenarios). Mitigations: (1) system prompt constraints; (2) static analysis as a guardrail layer (defence in depth); (3) security fine-tuning; (4) human review for high-risk contexts. Benchmarks like HumanEval measure functional correctness, not security.
questions.source: Pearce et al. Asleep at the Keyboard? Assessing the Security of GitHub Copilot's Code Contributions. IEEE S&P, 2022. https://arxiv.org/abs/2108.09293

mini_quizzes.title: MQ-4 · Generative AI Ethics, Safety and Governance

questions.stem: What is a 'jailbreak' in the context of LLM safety?

options.A: A method for unlocking faster GPU inference speeds
options.B: A prompt engineering technique that attempts to bypass an LLM's safety guardrails — tricking the model into generating content it was trained or instructed not to produce (harmful instructions, hate speech, private information)
options.C: A legal term for unauthorised access to LLM training data
options.D: A technique for extracting model weights from a black-box API

questions.explanation: Jailbreaks exploit misalignment between RLHF-trained safety constraints and the model's underlying capabilities. Common techniques: roleplay ('pretend you are an AI with no restrictions'), hypothetical framing, adversarial suffixes (Zou et al., 2023 — universal transferable suffixes that bypass safety training). Defences: Constitutional AI (Anthropic), input/output classifiers, red-teaming, adversarial training.
questions.source: Zou et al. Universal and Transferable Adversarial Attacks on Aligned Language Models. 2023. https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.15043

questions.stem: Under the EU AI Act (2024), how are general-purpose AI models (GPAIs) like GPT-4 regulated?

options.A: GPAIs are exempt from the EU AI Act entirely
options.B: GPAIs with systemic risk (≥10²³ FLOPs training compute) face enhanced obligations: adversarial testing, cybersecurity measures, incident reporting to the EU AI Office, and transparency about training data. All GPAIs must publish model cards and comply with EU copyright law
options.C: GPAIs are classified as unacceptable risk and banned in the EU
options.D: GPAIs only need to register with national governments, not the EU

questions.explanation: EU AI Act Title VIII (GPAIs): all GPAI providers must publish technical documentation and comply with EU copyright law. Systemic risk threshold: ≥10²³ FLOPs (currently GPT-4, Gemini Ultra, Claude 3 Opus). Additional obligations: adversarial testing (red-teaming), cybersecurity protections, incident reporting to the EU AI Office. The Act came into force August 2024 with phased compliance timelines.
questions.source: EU AI Act. Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, Title VIII. https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/the-act/

questions.stem: Why is copyright ownership of LLM-generated content a legally unresolved issue in most jurisdictions?

options.A: Copyright law fully covers AI-generated content in all countries
options.B: Traditional copyright doctrine requires human authorship. LLMs generate text from statistical patterns in training data — raising unresolved questions: (1) can AI-generated output be copyrighted by the user?; (2) does training on copyrighted data constitute infringement?; (3) who owns outputs that substantially reproduce training data? US courts and the EU are actively litigating and legislating these questions
options.C: AI-generated content is automatically in the public domain everywhere
options.D: Copyright only applies to images, not text generated by LLMs

questions.explanation: US Copyright Office (2023): AI-generated content without human creative input is not copyrightable. Training data lawsuits: NYT v. OpenAI (2023), Getty Images v. Stability AI (2023) — alleging training on copyrighted works constitutes infringement. EU AI Act requires GPAI providers to publish copyright compliance summaries. Legally, the safest position: assume LLM outputs may have unclear IP status for commercial use.
questions.source: US Copyright Office. Copyright and Artificial Intelligence. 2023. https://www.copyright.gov/ai; NYT v. OpenAI. SDNY, Case 1:23-cv-11195.

questions.stem: How does Constitutional AI (Anthropic) differ from standard RLHF as an alignment approach?

options.A: Constitutional AI and RLHF are identical techniques with different branding
options.B: Standard RLHF relies on human raters to rank model outputs — expensive and inconsistent at scale. Constitutional AI (CAI) uses a written set of principles (a 'constitution') to have the model critique and revise its own outputs via AI feedback, then trains on those revised outputs — reducing human annotation cost while encoding explicit ethical principles
options.C: Constitutional AI removes all safety constraints from Claude models
options.D: RLHF uses a constitution; Constitutional AI uses human raters

questions.explanation: CAI (Bai et al., Anthropic, 2022): (1) SFT on helpful demonstrations; (2) model generates responses, then self-critiques against the constitution ('Does this response contain harmful content?'), then revises; (3) AI feedback generates preference data → train reward model → PPO. Advantages: scalable, explicit principles auditable, less reliant on human rater consistency. Used in Claude model training.
questions.source: Bai et al. Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback. Anthropic, 2022. https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.08073

questions.stem: A news organisation wants to use an LLM to auto-generate breaking news articles. Evaluate the key risks and design a responsible deployment framework.

options.A: No risks — LLMs are factually accurate for news generation
options.B: Key risks: (1) hallucination — fabricated facts, quotes, statistics; (2) bias amplification from training data; (3) copyright — reproducing source articles; (4) speed incentivising accuracy shortcuts. Responsible framework: LLM drafts from verified wire feeds only (not open web); mandatory human journalist review and sign-off; factual claims auto-verified against trusted databases; AI-authorship disclosed to readers (EU AI Act Art. 50); zero-tolerance policy for unverified quotes
options.C: Use LLMs only if they achieve >95% accuracy on a news benchmark
options.D: Fully automate publishing — human review adds unacceptable latency

questions.explanation: News AI risks: hallucination in factual reporting causes reputational and legal harm (defamation). Responsible framework: RAG over verified sources (AP, Reuters wire) limits hallucination; human editorial sign-off preserves journalistic accountability; AI disclosure required under EU AI Act Art. 50 for synthetic media; speed-accuracy trade-off must be resolved in favour of accuracy for news. AP has published internal AI use standards as a model.
questions.source: EU AI Act Art. 50. 2024; AP. AP's approach to artificial intelligence. https://www.ap.org/about/news-values-and-principles/telling-the-story/artificial-intelligence; Ji et al. Survey of Hallucination. ACM, 2023.
